transcript.text: Hello, this is a book review of Slave Narratives, a folk history of slavery in the United States
From interviews with former slaves, administrative files, selected records varying on the history
Of slave narratives by Work Projects Administration.
It's 9,400 words.
I listened to it on fast speed in about three hours or something.
I don't know what it would be on slow speed.
I need to do the figurines.
But it has 17 chapters, Alabama, Arkansas, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, Maryland,
Mississippi, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, and Virginia.
And I didn't really distinguish the different states, but yeah, it's an interesting read.
I'm good to hear.
Interesting.
I got, there were just stories of really old slaves, a lot of really old people who've been around since before the slavery days.
And, you know, men and women.
And I'm kind of doing American history section right now.
I did the narrative of Frederick Douglass last week, and he was an escaped slave, and he only had negative things to say about slavery.
He said that, you know, the slaves who work in the plantation really have it bad.
But a lot of the slaves that I heard here, what I kind of took away from the book is, you know, a lot of these slaves actually liked slavery.
A lot of them, some of them were treated well in the plantation, and said that they, the slavery days were happier for the slaves, because slavery, because life was simpler.
Didn't have to make any decisions and stuff.
But, you know, some of the others didn't.
There was one story where a bunch of slaves, like, I think after they got their freedom, they wanted to kill, like, their white master and take their land or something.
But the other black people didn't agree with that, so they strung them up.
And a lot of, you know, a lot of, when they had to set their slaves free, they told them they set their slaves free.
And, you know, but on most occasions, the slaves just stayed and just received money.
One of the guys, he told his slaves that he'd have to set them free, because he didn't have the money to pay them anymore.
Because I guess the, he didn't have any money for, I don't know why, he didn't explain the economy was different.
But the slaves stayed, and they didn't really, see, a lot of slaves were really ignorant, because they weren't, they were separated from their parents, and they weren't allowed to read and stuff.
So they didn't even know what money was, so they just stayed, and they didn't even ask for money.
They just stayed and worked, you know, and it was just kind of like it was before, because they just had a nice owner who just, you know, they lived a simple life.
But, and a lot of the stories of the book, they would say how, like, how big they were.
They were like, you know, Jim Douglas, and like, they were little, like one guy, 5'3", 130 pounds, one guy, 5'6", 150 pounds, a woman, 5 feet even, 105 pounds, you know, 5'2", 110 pounds.
And, so, yeah, people back then were slender, they weren't fat like they are now, because they probably didn't eat that much.
They just worked all day long.
But I remember hearing in a lot of these stories that they complained that they weren't, that they're, you know, some of the owners just would whip them.
They'd get whipped all the time, you know, like if they were, because the owner thought that they were working too slowly in the field, so they'd get whipped.
And, and when they did this story, they actually used the language that they used, and it's interesting hearing their language, because they, they would, they said chidden, chidden, not children.
And the slaves had children, you know, and they kept, they kept in touch with their children, and, and, and had, you know, just like white people.
But, but, and what was some of the other dialects they had?
Oh, they used the N-word like, like it was just another word.
That was the only word they had for African American, was the N-word.
And they used it for themselves.
So back then, it wasn't, it wasn't a negative, negative connotation at all.
And I'm sure the white people, you know, would call them that, and it was, that's just how they called them, because it was the only word that they had.
So I, I'd like to read about the history of that word, and see when blacks decided that they didn't like it, and it meant, like, a derogatory word.
But, because it sure wasn't back in these days.
This book was written in, uh, 1941, so, I don't know when the actual, uh, stories were recorded.
But, uh, yeah, a lot of the people, it was just, you know, really old people, just telling their story.
And, uh, you know, just, like once, and then talking about the Civil War, that one of them sat there and saw a bunch of Union soldiers walk by in their blue outfits.
And, and they didn't, uh, do anything to the slaves.
They didn't, like, hurt them or anything, but they, or they didn't steal anything from their house or anything.
They just didn't pay any attention to them.
But they, uh, they killed a cow and fed themselves with it.
And, um, so, uh, yeah, that's about what I took away from it.
That's about as much as you can take away from a, from, I don't even, from a 9,400 word book.
I don't know how many pages that is.
It's not recorded here.
Um, I don't have it memorized yet.
And about while you're riding your bike, paying attention to other things.
